event: Early hominins such as Australopithecus africanus inhabit the region, later evidenced by fossils like the Taung Child and Sterkfontein finds in the Cradle of Humankind.
Anatomically modern humans occupy southern Africa, leaving early evidence of symbolic behavior at sites such as Blombos Cave and Pinnacle Point.
San hunter-gatherers create extensive rock art across the region, part of one of the world's longest continuous artistic traditions.
Khoikhoi pastoralists herding sheep and cattle spread through the western and southern Cape.
Bantu-speaking farmers practicing ironworking and agriculture settle in the eastern and northern parts of present-day South Africa.
The trading kingdom of Mapungubwe rises in the Limpopo Valley, becoming southern Africa's first known state with gold wealth and Indian Ocean trade links.
Mapungubwe declines as power shifts north to Great Zimbabwe, with trade networks reorienting beyond the Limpopo.
Portuguese navigator Bartolomeu Dias rounds the Cape of Good Hope, the first European to do so.
Vasco da Gama sails along the South African coast en route to India, naming Natal.
Jan van Riebeeck establishes a Dutch East India Company refreshment station at the Cape of Good Hope, founding Cape Town.
The first enslaved people are imported to the Cape from West Africa and Asia, beginning a long history of slavery in the colony.
French Huguenot refugees arrive at the Cape, strengthening the settler population and establishing the wine industry.
The first of the Cape Frontier Wars breaks out between Dutch settlers and Xhosa peoples along the eastern frontier.
Britain seizes the Cape Colony from the Dutch during the French Revolutionary Wars.
Britain reoccupies the Cape after the Battle of Blaauwberg, securing permanent control of the colony.
Shaka becomes king of the Zulu, forging a powerful military kingdom during the upheaval known as the Mfecane.
Around 4,000 British settlers arrive in the Eastern Cape, expanding the colony's English-speaking population.
Slavery is abolished in the Cape Colony, prompting discontent among Boer slaveholders.
The Great Trek begins as Boer Voortrekkers migrate inland to escape British rule, founding new republics.
Voortrekkers defeat the Zulu at the Battle of Blood River after the assassination of leader Piet Retief.
Britain recognizes the independence of the Boer-ruled South African Republic (Transvaal) under the Sand River Convention.
The Orange Free State is established as an independent Boer republic.
Diamonds are discovered near Kimberley, triggering a mining rush and transforming the regional economy.
The Anglo-Zulu War sees the British defeated at Isandlwana before ultimately conquering the Zulu Kingdom.
Gold is discovered on the Witwatersrand, leading to the founding of Johannesburg and a massive economic boom.
The Second Anglo-Boer War erupts between the British Empire and the Boer republics.
The Treaty of Vereeniging ends the Anglo-Boer War, bringing the Boer republics under British rule.
The Union of South Africa is formed, unifying the Cape, Natal, Transvaal, and Orange Free State as a British dominion.
The South African Native National Congress, later the African National Congress (ANC), is founded to defend Black rights.
The Natives Land Act restricts Black land ownership to a small fraction of the country, entrenching racial dispossession.
South Africa gains effective legislative independence from Britain through the Statute of Westminster.
South Africa becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
The National Party wins power and begins implementing apartheid, the formal system of racial segregation.
Police kill 69 protesters at Sharpeville, prompting international condemnation and the banning of the ANC and PAC.
South Africa becomes a republic and withdraws from the Commonwealth amid criticism of apartheid.
Nelson Mandela and other anti-apartheid leaders are sentenced to life imprisonment at the Rivonia Trial.
The Soweto uprising erupts as students protest Afrikaans-language instruction, met with deadly police force.
Western nations impose comprehensive economic sanctions on South Africa to pressure the apartheid government.
President F. W. de Klerk unbans the ANC and frees Nelson Mandela after 27 years in prison.
South Africa holds its first democratic, multiracial elections and Nelson Mandela becomes president, ending apartheid.
The Truth and Reconciliation Commission begins hearings, and South Africa hosts and wins the Rugby World Cup.
A new post-apartheid Constitution with a celebrated Bill of Rights is adopted.
Thabo Mbeki succeeds Mandela as president following the ANC's electoral victory.
South Africa becomes a founding member of the African Union, which is launched in Durban.
South Africa becomes the first African nation to host the FIFA World Cup.
Nelson Mandela dies at age 95, mourned in a global outpouring of tribute.
Cyril Ramaphosa becomes president after Jacob Zuma resigns amid corruption scandals.
The ANC loses its parliamentary majority for the first time since 1994, leading to a government of national unity.
